data.title: BlackRock TCP Capital: Still Too Speculative To Go Long
data.description:
In early 2024, I managed to identify some specific areas within BlackRock TCP Capital Corp. (TCPC) that were experiencing a notable buildup of risks. My main concern was linked to the weakening portfolio quality and emerging non-accruals. Even though the base dividend coverage was still relatively solid and the debt-to-equity level was just slightly above the 1.0x mark, considering the lack of a meaningful discount, I decided to rotate out of TCPC. Since then, TCPC has registered a negative total return of ~15% and a negative price return of almost 33%. At the same time, the BDC market (BIZD) has gone up. So, in terms of alpha generation, TCPC has been a disastrous investment.

However, what we have now is a heavily discounted TCPC that has already made some relatively deep dividend cuts. Currently, TCPC trades at an 18% discount to NAV, offering one of the highest dividend yields in the sector - 15.5%.

Given the backing of an institutional giant, BlackRock (BLK), it might theoretically be so that there is now an opportunity to pick TCPC up, while probably many investors have assumed a quite bearish stance against it (due to the recent underperformance and value destruction as I mentioned above).

Let's take a closer look at this case to see whether it is indeed an interesting BDC bargain to include in the portfolio mix.

Thesis review

To see what things look like for TCPC, it is worth exploring the recently published Q2 2025 financial data.

You will not like the start. The Q2 2025 adjusted NII per share came in at $0.31, which is $0.05 per share below the result achieved in the previous quarter. The main driver for the drop was clearly the reduction in the total investment income, where TCPC had to recognize full writedowns of several loans that were already labeled as underperforming ones. On top of these eliminations, there were four incremental non-accruals that TCPC had to recognize in Q2, which stimulated not only NII per share decline but also a clear decrease in the NAV statistic.

Apart from the TCPC-specific negatives, the BDC had to deal with the overall sector headwinds that are especially relevant for the middle and upper market first-lien players. Namely, the weighted average annual effective yield of TCPC's portfolio declined by 20 basis points on a quarter-to-quarter basis, landing at exactly 12.0% as of Q2 2025. And what really illustrates the issue is the fact that the new investments that were made during Q2 were underwritten at 10.8% yields - i.e., still a notable gap between the portfolio in-place yields and what can be sourced from the market now.

This portfolio vs. market yield difference is something that we just have to factor in when thinking about TCPC's future NII per share generation. There is nothing that TCPC can do to mitigate the issue (pressure) unless it changes the investment strategy from a relatively defensive one to, say, a second-lien focused one. From the new investment funding, we cannot draw such a conclusion. So, all things equal, TCPC's NII per share is set to go down.

Unfortunately, I have a couple of additional items, which, in my view, will also contribute to declining results.

First, the base rates are very likely trending downwards, already this September. As most of TCPC's loans are linked to floating rates, we can easily expect similar pressures as in the Q4 2024 - Q1 2025 period (i.e., a period when the BDCs experienced headwinds from the Fed interest rate cuts across the board).

Second, in early 2026, TCPC will have to roll over a $325 million fixed rate debt that has been sourced at 2.85% - a significant difference from the prevailing fixed rate financing rates. This will certainly inflate the interest expense component for TCPC.

Third, TCPC ended the Q2 at a debt-to-equity of 1.42x, which is definitely one of the highest leverage levels in the sector. It does not mean that TCPC will suffer additional value destruction from this fact alone, but it for sure implies that the growth prospects are very limited due to largely exhausted debt capacity.

Fourth, and this is quite important in the context of the current discount, TCPC's exposure to PIK interest income is considerable. In Q2 2025, about 11.4% of the total investment income came from PIK streams, which means that the actual cash generation for TCPC is much lower than what the adjusted NII per share indicates.

Fifth, the Q2 figures included a temporary boost from the partially waived base management fees that will be in place until Q3 2025 (inclusive). After Q3, we have to start calculating higher management fees.

Now, to be more balanced in my argument, I will also spell out some positives that I see here.

Besides the notable discount and rich dividend yield, the rate of change in both PIK and non-accruals has been encouraging. For example, the PIK share has declined by 20 basis points from the last quarter. Similarly, the non-accrual rate has also come down for the third quarter in a row - to 3.7% from 4.4% and 5.6% for Q1 2025 and Q4 2024, respectively (all measured on a fair value basis).

Another major positive for TCPC is that the current base dividend coverage is 124%, which puts the BDC in the sector's Top 10 statistic (in terms of dividend coverage strength). Plus, the excess cash flows have been directed to the share repurchases (i.e., 40,830 shares repurchased in Q2), which from the capital allocation perspective makes sense.

The bottom line

Now, if I put all of these puzzle pieces together, I have to say that TCPC remains an extremely speculative case. Even though the current situation looks somewhat acceptable - i.e., improving picture, deep discount, and high dividend coverage - the direction of travel in the adjusted NII per share generation is clearly negative. So, what we see in the broad statistics is only temporary and subject to deterioration going forward.

In this context, the 18% discount does not seem that attractive.

In my opinion, if the Fed cuts more than 50 basis points and/or there are more non-accruals coming online, then we will be most probably talking about yet another dividend cut from TCPC.

data.title: Walker And Dunlop Tries To Stay Healthy In A Struggling Market
data.description:
Walker and Dunlop (NYSE: WD) released its Q2 2025 earnings before the market opened on August 7. Prior to the announcement, the company’s stock had lost over 22% year-to-date, but the positive news from the commercial lender’s most recent quarter has given it a boost of about 13%. General market headwinds in commercial real estate have been challenging for WD over the last year, despite the company's previous decade of success, where it has experienced compound average growth in revenues of 12% and annual increases in net income of 18%.

Even with the strong recent earnings report, WD is trading well above its 10-year average PE, and the company may have some macro headwinds to battle in the final half of this year. The company has a strong record of previous success and an above average dividend yield. These things were not enough to persuade me to give the stock a Buy rating at this time.

Company Overview

Walker and Dunlop was founded in 1937 and is currently based in Bethesda, Maryland. The company is one of the world’s largest commercial real estate finance and advisory companies. Walker and Dunlop was the top lender for Fannie Mae’s Delegated Underwriting and Servicing program in 2024 and third in overall government-sponsored enterprise loans.

When reporting its financial results, Walker and Dunlop separates revenues and expenses into three segments – Capital Markets, Servicing and Asset Management, and Corporate. A description of each of these segments is as follows:

  • Capital Markets – Through this division, the company provides agency lending, debt brokerage, property sales, and appraisals. WD also provides real estate-related investment banking and advisory services.
  • Servicing and Asset Management – Activities for this segment include servicing the company’s loan portfolio as well as managing third-party capital invested in commercial real estate and tax credit equity funds.
  • Corporate – This division consists primarily of WD’s treasury operations and other corporate “support functions”.

With a market cap of $2.86 billion, WD ended last year with a loan servicing portfolio of over $135 billion, with $30 billion in debt origination in 2024. Last year, the company managed a transaction volume of $40 billion and brought in revenues of almost $1.1 billion. Of that revenue total, the largest share, about 52.2%, came from Servicing and Asset Management. Just over 46.3% came from Capital Markets.

WD has a dividend yield of 3.12% which is just slightly ahead of the median for its sector. Assuming it makes its payment in Q4, this will be the seventh consecutive year that the company has raised its payout. The five-year dividend growth rate for Walker and Dunlop is 14.9%.

Analysis of Walker and Dunlop

Analysis of the earnings for Walker and Dunlop is more challenging than for other companies because the bottom line for the company can be impacted significantly by the timing of various transactions. On first glance, the company’s diluted earnings per share have declined significantly over the last three years. After earning $6.36 per share in FY 2022, WD reported diluted profits of $3.18 per share in FY 2023 and $3.19 in FY 2024.

However, a closer look at some of the individual line items on the company’s consolidated financial statements paint a somewhat clearer, and more positive picture. Servicing fees have increased every year since 2022, starting at $300.2 million and ending at $325.6 million last year. Total revenues for Servicing & Asset Management were $507.4 million in FY 2022, but they grew to $563.8 million in FY 2023 and $591.6 million last year. The company has also been able to lower its overall expenses in the last three years, from a total of $607.4 million in 2022 when all three divisions were combined to $559.2 million in 2024.

Walker and Dunlop’s Capital Markets division was able to benefit greatly from the interest rate environment in 2022 as the company reported earnings of $4.64 per share in that business segment alone. In 2023 and 2024, Capital Markets combined to make only $3.19. These declines appear to be correcting themselves somewhat this year, while still not back at the levels seen in 2022. WD’s Capital Markets division reported diluted EPS of $0.97 in Q2 2025, a 194% improvement over the $0.33 earned in the same quarter last year. A complete breakdown of the results from the Capital Markets division from the most recent earnings announcement from the company can be seen below.

The other two divisions did not experience similar improvements, however. Servicing and Asset Management reported earnings of $1.10 per share after earning $1.19 in Q2 2024. The Corporate segment lost $1.08 per share after losing only $0.85 in the same quarter last year. For the Servicing division, the company reported lower fee income because of a reduction in the accrual for management fees from its low-income housing tax credit funds. The Corporate segment profits slipped because of an increase in personnel salaries and benefits.

The outlook for the company in the second half of 2025 is good, but not great. The company is expecting to record gains in the "high single-digits to double-digits" in diluted EPS. Adjusted core EPS, which removes non-recurring items, is expected to be flat to high-single digits even though the comparison has been negative in the first six months.

With any company that is heavily involved in commercial finance, debt should be an important part of an investment thesis. Walker and Dunlop’s ratings from S&P and Moody’s are straddling the line between investment grade and slightly speculative as shown below. The most recent outlook offered by both agencies is stable. On July 8 of this year, Fitch Ratings affirmed WD’s commercial primary servicer rating at CPS1-, which is a confirmation of the company’s servicing capability, not its creditworthiness. Again, the picture here looks good, but not great.

Market Outlook for Commercial Real Estate

Walker and Dunlop has an elevated exposure to commercial real estate and commercial mortgages. Multiple sources have published articles this year that predict an impending crisis for CRE lenders. Major issues of concern include increased default rates, decreased property values, and liquidity constraints. Because some of this news has been disseminated in the market for some time, I was surprised to find WD trading at such an elevated PE ratio when compared to its five-year average. The stock is trading below its price to book average, according to Morningstar, so the current share price is not quite as out of line as the PE ratio may imply.

Most concerning for me is that the market headwinds for Walker and Dunlop may get worse before they get better, putting further pressure on its valuation. The Mortgage Bankers Association noted an increase in delinquencies for commercial properties in Q1 of this year. Declines in economic growth in the second half of 2025 could cause further advancement in this trend.

In addition to delinquency concerns, commercial real estate is also dealing with higher interest rates and a wave of refinancing that is coming due in the next three years. Approximately $600 billion in commercial loans will mature on an annual basis between 2025 and 2028. Property insurance costs have risen, often faster than rental income. Vacancy rates for office properties remain high, with the average in most major cities ranging from 16 to 20%.

To its credit, Walker and Dunlop is trying to adjust to the demands of the market. Its Capital Markets division is expanding into new opportunities with hospitality and data centers. The company is also expanding into projects in Europe. While markets in Europe appear relatively healthy now, they have traditionally trailed the US in overall economic growth.

In its latest earnings report, the company also pointed out opportunity in multifamily housing, another key source for loan origination and servicing. With housing near all-time highs for adjusted costs, renting has become incredibly affordable on a relative basis. As of May of this year, renting offers a monthly savings of almost $540 per month over a typical home mortgage with principal and interest. This disparity should help keep occupancy rates high, increasing the quality and likelihood of payback for those loans.

Additional Risks to Consider

Walker and Dunlop relies heavily on Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac loan programs for origination volume. As I mentioned in my company overview, the company was the top lender for Fannie Mae’s DUS program and third in overall government-sponsored enterprise loans. Regulatory changes to these two programs could directly impact WD’s main sources of revenue. The Trump administration has announced that it may take both Fannie and Freddie public as soon as this year.

Walker and Dunlop’s CEO, Willy Walker, wrote about the potential consequences of this move back in March of this year. It is possible that privatization could cause a disruption in the mortgage market that would ultimately raise costs for borrowers. One estimation from Walker’s article stated that mortgage rates “could increase by 20-40 basis points due to uncertainty and new capital requirements”. He added, “We are concerned that a rushed privatization of Fannie Mae could undermine market confidence without an implicit or explicit government guarantee.” Additional risks for Walker and Dunlop from this scenario could include added pressure on margins, tighter rate spreads, and reduced market share.

The Bottom Line

My overall impression of Walker and Dunlop after concluding my research is that the company is well-managed based on its track record of success. My concerns about the current valuation of the stock and the headwinds that are still impacting its main sources of income are too much to push the needle over toward Buy for this stock at this time. I also think that the current debate about the privatization of Fannie and Freddie is worth watching as it relates to WD’s future business opportunities.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system appears to agree with my assessment of the company, giving the stock a “C-” in Valuation and a “D-” in Profitability. The stock has actually been in the Sell range for much of the last year. Further inspection into WD’s Valuation on Seeking Alpha reveals that the stock gets grades of “D” in PE Non-GAAP, PE GAAP, and PEG GAAP when compared to its sector.

data.title: Powell Pivot Sparks REIT Rebound
data.description:
Real Estate Weekly Outlook

U.S. equity markets notched another series of record highs this week, surging into the weekend after surprisingly dovish commentary from Federal Reserve Chair Powell, who acknowledged the risk of the Fed's currently restrictive policy stance and hinted at a likely resumption of rate cuts next month. Powell used his final Jackson Hole speech as Fed Chair to deliver a clear policy pivot, stating “the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance" - an unexpected reversal after months of insistence that forecasted tariff-related inflation warranted a more hawkish approach. Markets were equally relieved by the policy-focused nature of Powell's speech amid speculation that the address may be used instead as a potential defiant sermon on central bank independence as a means to rebuke recent White House criticism.

Rallying after Powell's pivot to erase early-week declines, the S&P 500 climbed 0.2% this week, closing fractionally below fresh all-time record-highs. The gains were more pronounced across the smaller-cap benchmarks, which have been lagging their large-cap, tech-heavy peers to a historic degree since the start of the Fed's hiking cycle in early 2022. The Small-Cap 600 surged 3.4% this week, while the Mid-Cap 400 gained 2.6%, far outpacing the mega-cap Nasdaq 100, which declined 1.0%. Real estate equities - the most "Fed sensitive" market segment - naturally led the gains this week, as rate relief appears to finally be in sight after three years of battling stiff headwinds. The Equity REIT Index rallied 2.8% this week, with 17-of-18 property sectors in positive territory, while the Mortgage REIT Index advanced 2.1%. Homebuilders and the broader Housing Index also delivered strong gains as 30-Year Mortgage Rates dipped to the lowest levels since last October.

Benchmark interest rates tumbled during and after Powell's speech as traders interpreted the Chairman's focus on employment risks and comments on "adjusting" the policy stance as a clear signal that the Fed is leaning towards a rate cut at its mid-September meeting. Dipping to four-month lows, the policy-sensitive 2-Year Treasury Yield fell by 6 basis points this week to 3.69% - down sharply from the recent highs of 3.95% late last month. Providing some added support at the long end of the curve, S&P Global Ratings affirmed its "AA+" credit rating on the U.S. this week, noting the "meaningful" revenue generated from new tariffs will help to offset the fiscal hit from recent tax and spending legislation. The 10-Year Treasury Yield dipped by 6 basis points this week to close at 4.25%, trading toward the lower end of its three-month range between 4.20% in early August and 4.60% in mid-May. Swaps markets now imply a Fed Funds Rate of 3.95% at the end of 2025 (vs. 4.50% now), implying 2.2 Fed interest rate cuts in 2025 - roughly unchanged on the week. The odds of a September rate cut, meanwhile, stand at roughly 83%. Meanwhile, WTI Crude Oil rebounded from two-month lows this week after the meeting between President Trump and Russian President Putin failed to yield an immediate breakthrough in peace negotiations.

Real Estate Economic Data

Below, we recap the most important economic data points over this past week affecting the residential and commercial real estate marketplace.

Equity REIT & Homebuilder Week In Review

Best & Worst Performance This Week Across the REIT Sector

Industrial: Are the animal spirits starting to come alive in the real estate sector? Plymouth Industrial (PLYM) - which we've owned in the REIT Dividend Growth Portfolio - soared 41% this week after it received a takeover offer from asset manager Sixth Street Partners, which offered to buy PLYM for $24.10 per share in cash - a 65% premium over its prior closing price. PLYM - a small-cap industrial REIT with a market cap of approx $650M before today's surge - owns 148 properties comprising over 30M square feet, primarily in the Midwest and Southeastern U.S., dubbed the "golden triangle." CEO Jeffrey Witherell joined the Hoya Hotseat earlier this year to discuss PLYM's strategy. Sixth Street already owns 10% of PLYM common shares and owns additional interest via its strategic partnership announced last year, in which it provided $250M to PLYM in exchange for preferred stock and a joint venture interest in its Chicago portfolio - a deal that was criticized by analysts for its complexity and cost. PLYM - which went public in 2017 - had generally been performing in line with its industrial-focused peers over a 3-year and 5-year basis, but had underperformed since the partnership was announced last August. Plymouth announced that its board plans to evaluate the offer with its advisers to determine whether the deal serves shareholders’ interests.

Healthcare: Ventas (VTR) - the second-largest senior housing REIT - traded flat this week after Bloomberg reported that it will acquire a $600M portfolio of six Long Island-based senior communities from Harrison Street Asset Management - its largest transaction this year. Senior housing operator B2K - which operates 24 senior housing facilities under The Bristal brand - will continue to manage the properties and can earn incentive fees based on performance. Ventas CIO Justin Hutchens confirmed to Bloomberg, noting the portfolio is comprised of "high-quality senior living communities with upside potential." Ventas and its larger peer Welltower (WELL) have been among the most active REITs on the acquisitions front during this past three-year "bear market" for commercial real estate, a time that has seen historically low levels of REIT acquisition activity. Including this recent deal, Ventas has acquired nearly $2 billion in senior housing properties YTD, while Welltower announced last month that it has acquired $9.2 billion in properties YTD - combined accounting for 42% of the $26.0B in total acquisitions across the entire REIT sector this year. Riding the post-pandemic senior housing revival, VTR and WELL are two of the only REITs whose shares have traded at sizable and consistent premiums to their consensus Net Asset Value ("NAV") in recent years - a critical "cost of capital" gauge enabling accretive acquisitions.

Mortgage: Residential mREIT Two Harbors (TWO) declined 3% this week after it announced that it agreed to pay $375M in cash to settle long-running litigation related to the April 2020 termination of its external manager Pine River Capital Management ("PRCM"). Back in 2020, TWO's Board voted to terminate its external management agreement with PRCM "for cause," citing material breaches and gross negligence, thus avoiding a roughly $140M termination fee. The termination came after TWO had plunged more than 70% over the preceding month amid the early pandemic turmoil, worse than the 50% plunge in the broader Mortgage REIT Index in that time. A five-year legal battle ensued, with PRCM seeking payment of the fee with interest plus excess damages related to claims that TWO poached its employees and stole its intellectual property. In May, a judge found that Two Harbors did not have a basis to terminate the agreement for cause, prompting TWO to record a $198.9M contingency liability to cover the termination fee. While the $375M settlement was on the higher-end of analyst estimates, it was below the nearly $500M in damages that PRCM was seeking. In exchange for the payment, Pine River will relinquish all ownership claims to IP, ending the long-running dispute. TWO noted that after the payment, its book value per share ("BVPS") is $11.06 per common share, still roughly 12% above its closing share price.

Alongside the announced settlement, Two Harbors (TWO) also announced that it would reduce its dividend for the second time this year - one of sixteen REITs that have lowered their dividends this year. TWO declared a $0.34/share quarterly dividend (13.4% dividend yield), a 13% decrease from its prior dividend of $0.39. On the upside this week, industrial REIT Eastgroup Properties (EGP) rallied 4% after raising its quarterly dividend by 11% to $1.55/share (3.7% dividend yield), becoming the 54th REIT to raise its dividend this year. Among the most significant dividend increases this year, EGP becomes one of 17 REITs that have raised their payout by double-digit percentage points. The market-cap weighted Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) currently pays a dividend yield of 3.8%, but an equal-weight basket of the 190 U.S. REITs produces an average dividend yield of 6.1%, a function of the premium yields available among small and mid-cap REITs.

Additional Headlines from The Daily REITBeat on iREIT+Hoya

  • Highwoods Properties (HIW) acquired the Legacy Union Parking Garage in Charlotte, North Carolina for $111.5M. The 3,057-space garage supports the 1.2M square feet of Highwoods-owned office at Legacy Union.

  • CTO Realty (CTO) sold its Main Street properties in Daytona Beach, FL for $7.1 million, which generated a gain on sale of approximately $1.1 million. As part of the transaction, the Company provided seller financing of $5.0 million for 5 years at an annual rate of 6.50%.

  • NexPoint Residential (NXRT) announced the dual listing of its common stock on the NYSE Texas effective August 19, 2025.

  • City Office (CIO) completed the first closing in the sale of its Phoenix portfolio for gross sale proceeds of $266M, which represents six of its seven properties. Its Pima Center property continues to be under contract at a $30M gross sales price, which is expected to close at a later date.

REIT Capital Raising

  • Braemar Hotels (BHR) refinanced the existing mortgage loan secured by the Four Seasons Resort Scottsdale at Troon North. The new non-recourse loan has a balance of $180M and bears interest of SOFR + 3.00%.

  • Essentials Properties (EPRT) raised $400M via a public offering of ten-year senior unsecured notes due 2035 at a 5.40% interest rate.

  • Redwood Trust (RWT) raised $50M via a direct placement of two-year convertible senior notes due 2027 at a 7.75% interest rate. The Notes were issued as a reopening of the 7.75% convertible senior notes due 2027 that the Company originally issued in June 2022.

  • Easterly Government (DEA) upsized its unsecured term loan agreement to $200M from its original total of $175M. The term loan will continue to bear interest at a rate of SOFR plus 1.20%-1.70%.

REIT Credit Ratings

  • Fitch Ratings affirmed the credit ratings of Essentials Property Trust (EPRT) at “BBB," including its Issuer Default Rating and its senior unsecured debt rating, and revised its outlook to positive from stable.

2025 Performance & 2024 Recap

Nearing the end of summer, real estate equities have posted mixed performance compared to the major equity benchmarks, slumping during the Spring and early Summer, but showing renewed signs of life in recent weeks on hopes of imminent Fed rate cuts. The Equity REIT Index is now higher by 5.5% on the year, while the Mortgage REIT Index has gained 9.4%, and the Housing 100 Index has advanced 6.9%. This compares with the 10.7% advance for the S&P 500, the 5.1% gain for the Mid-Cap 400, and the 3.5% gain for the Small-Cap 600. Within the REIT sector, 10-of-18 property sectors are higher on the year, led by Healthcare, Cell Tower, and Net Lease REITs, while downside laggards have included Cannabis, Data Center, and Farmland REITs. At 4.25%, the 10-Year Treasury Yield is now lower by 32 basis points on the year, while the 2-Year Treasury Yield is lower by 55 basis points at 3.69%. The aggregate Bloomberg US Bond Index is now higher by 4.8% this year, led by outperformance from mid-duration Treasuries and high-yield credit, offsetting some weakness at the longer end of the maturity curve.

In commodities markets, WTI Crude Oil is now lower by 1.2% this year - retreating over the past two months after surging to six-month highs in June - but the broader Commodities complex remains higher by 6.0%, buoyed by a 28% surge in Gold prices. The Bloomberg Commodity ex-Precious Metals Index is lower by 2.2% on the year. In currency markets, the US Dollar Index (DXY) has declined 5.2% this year - hovering around the lowest levels since 2022 - after appreciating by roughly 7% last year. Looking back on 2024, the Equity REIT Index posted modest total returns of 4.8%, while the Mortgage REIT Index posted total returns of 1.6% - significantly lagging the 26.2% gain on the S&P 500. Within the REIT sector, 12 of the 18 property sectors finished higher last year, led by Data Center, Regional Mall, Healthcare, and Office REITs, while "goods-oriented" sectors generally lagged, with Timber, Industrial, and Cannabis REITs among the losers. In 2024, the aggregate Bloomberg US Bond Index ultimately posted modest total returns of roughly 1% on the year, while the Commodities complex gained about 6%.

Economic Calendar In The Week Ahead

We'll see another busy slate of economic data and "Fed Speak" in the week ahead. The main event comes on Friday via the PCE Price Index - the Fed's preferred gauge of inflation - which is expected to tick higher to 2.6% in July, a third straight monthly uptick after hitting four-year lows of 2.1% in April. Earlier this month, the CPI and PPI showed that while consumer inflation has remained surprisingly muted, producers have started to feel the pinch of tariffs. Housing markets will be in focus earlier in the week - a sector that is likely to show renewed signs of life in the coming months as the Fed resumes its rate-cutting cycle. After hovering around 7% for much of this year, the 30-Year Fixed Mortgage Rate dipped to 6.58% last week - the lowest since October 2024. On Monday, we'll see New Home Sales, which is expected to show a sales pace of 630k in July as builders continued to aggressively discount inventory via price cuts and rate buydowns to counter affordability headwinds. On Tuesday, the Case Shiller Home Price Index is expected to show that home price appreciation cooled to 2.2% in June - the slowest since mid-2023 when prices briefly turned negative before reaccelerating through early 2024. On Thursday, we'll see Pending Home Sales, which is expected to show that existing sales activity remained near three-decade lows in July. We'll also be watching revisions to the second-quarter Gross Domestic Product, which is expected to confirm the 3.0% annualized increase reported in the initial read -fueled largely by a rebound in net exports after a pre-tariff surge in imports resulted in a negative GDP reading in the first quarter.

For an in-depth analysis of all real estate sectors, check out all of our quarterly reports: Apartments, Homebuilders, Manufactured Housing, Student Housing, Single-Family Rentals, Cell Towers, Casinos, Industrial, Data Center, Malls, Healthcare, Net Lease, Shopping Centers, Hotels, Billboards, Office, Farmland, Storage, Timber, Mortgage, and Cannabis.

Disclosure: Hoya Capital Real Estate advises two Exchange-Traded Funds listed on the NYSE. In addition to any long positions listed below, Hoya Capital is long all components in the Hoya Capital Housing 100 Index and in the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index. Index definitions and a complete list of holdings are available on our website.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Jackson Hole: Investors Missed Powell's Note About The Neutral Interest Rate
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this week, leading economists and Central Bank figures gathered in Jackson Hole, Wyoming for the annual economic symposium. The event was highlighted Friday morning when Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell gave remarks on the state of the economy. While his speech struck a dovish note and caused markets to rally, I believe that investors may have missed the message regarding the Federal Reserve’s long-term rate path.

Comments on Economic Growth

The least discussed aspect of Powell’s speech centered around economic growth. Despite growth being reduced to almost a footnote, it is important for investors to realize that during the first half of 2025, economic growth slowed to half of what it was a year earlier. At its current pace, economic growth is slated to come in par with 2022 and possibly be at levels not seen since 2020. Powell did warn that “ over the longer run, changes in tax, spending, and regulatory policies may also have important implications for economic growth and productivity.”

The Federal Reserve does believe that below trend (which is 1.8%) real growth is likely to continue through the end of next year. In its most recent economic projections, the Fed lowered its growth forecast to 1.4% for 2025, 1.6% for 2026, and maintained its long-term target of 1.8% for 2027. Based on the speech in its entirety, I would not be surprised to see the Fed lower its 2026 growth estimates in the September meeting.

Comments on Inflation

The Federal Reserve’s rate policy in 2022 and 2023 had more to do with inflation than employment, and many have been placing more emphasis on inflation as a result. Chairman Powell noted that inflation has progressed well since the problems in 2022, but that risks remain “tilted to the upside.”

As an exhibit, Powell introduced an inflation chart that included core goods, housing services, and core services less housing. The chart shows that core services inflation remains stubborn as housing services inflation continues to fall. Core goods inflation is at its highest level in two years, but at 1%, coming out of an extended deflationary run.

Powell did indicate that survey data showed inflation on a steadier path to 2 percent by stating that “ measures of longer-term inflation expectations, however, as reflected in market- and survey-based measures, appear to remain well anchored and consistent with our longer-run inflation objective of 2 percent.” This admission was somewhat surprising as the Fed’s core inflation estimates for 2026 steadily rose from 2.0% last September to 2.4% in the June meeting. The Fed also expects core inflation to remain above target into 2027. I’m going to be very curious to see where the committee pegs core inflation in its September meeting, which should also include 2028 projections.

The Labor Market: Steadily Quiet

While the economy remains at full employment (4.2% unemployment rate), the steep decline in the revisions of the last jobs report is on the mind of the Federal Reserve, considering there was an exhibit specifically pointing out the drop. Despite the downward revisions, Powell remains confident that the current state of the labor market is in balance by stating that “ labor supply has softened in line with demand, sharply lowering the "breakeven" rate of job creation needed to hold the unemployment rate constant.”

While the labor market continues to outperform the Fed’s economic expectations, the balance that has been struck based on less overall activity has the committee acting cautious. Powell stated that “ overall, while the labor market appears to be in balance, it is a curious kind of balance that results from a marked slowing in both the supply of and demand for workers. This unusual situation suggests that downside risks to employment are rising. And if those risks materialize, they can do so quickly in the form of sharply higher layoffs and rising unemployment.”

The Fed’s unemployment projections paint a slightly more pessimistic tone with unemployment expected to be higher than current levels for 2025, 2026, and 2027. The 2026 estimate will be key in the September meeting to see if the Fed expects more rapid weakness (by raising the unemployment expectation) or not.

Converting The Data to Rate Policy

At the end of the day, Powell stated that inflation was heading higher and that the labor market was heading lower, putting the Central Bank in a tough position. The Central Bank did have a plan, however, as Powell stated that “ When our goals are in tension like this, our framework calls for us to balance both sides of our dual mandate.” He added that, “with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance.”

Powell’s comments essentially stated that the committee likely needed to move towards the neutral rate, which is lower than the current position, translating into a rate cut. This is the portion of the speech that likely churned the markets higher and brought optimism that the September meeting would bring a rate cut, but investors may have missed the bigger picture.

The Outlook for Long-Term Rates

While the corks were popping over the possibility of a rate cut at next month's meeting, investors may have missed an important message regarding long-term rates. Over the past year, the Federal Reserve has slowly moved the neutral (or long-term target) rate upwards. In less than two years, the long-term rate target has moved up from 2.5% to 3.0%. Powell shared an exhibit showing the trend in long term target rates and the tendency of the committee seems to be pointing higher than 3% with the upward band extending further than the downward band.

These comments are especially important considering Fed fund futures are trading at nearly 50 basis points below where the Fed is projecting the end of 2026, meaning the markets are expecting two more rate cuts over the next fifteen months than the Fed is projecting. With the next meeting, the Fed could easily implement a 25-basis point cut, increase its 2025 year end estimates by 25 basis points, and increase its long-term rate projection. This would essentially amount to the Fed giving a rate cut today and permanently taking one away by moving the neutral rate higher.

Conclusion

While Chairman Powell’s Jackson Hole speech laid out the path to an immediate rate cut, the long-term path is more complicated. Ongoing inflation concerns have caused the Central Bank to gradually push up its neutral rate forecast. Like they did with cuts, the mention of neutral rate outlook in Powell’s speech should lead investors to believe the long-term rate target should rise accordingly. The market may not want to acclimate itself to rate cuts, even if the Fed eases next month.

data.title: Fairlight Alpha Fund Q2 2025 Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners,

Fairlight Alpha Fund’s second quarter 2025 returns were 9.3% net of fees. This compares to 10.9% for the S&P 500 Total Return (SP500TR) index. Overall, since inception the fund has returned 684.6% net of fees and 38.4% on an annualized basis. These results are presented for informational purposes only and do not represent a solicitation or offer.

Performance vs. the S&P 500

We track the net asset value of Fairlight Alpha Fund (the “Fund”) over time as calculated by our fund administrator based on our portfolio positions and prices over time on a dollar basis. The fund financials are also audited each year by an independent auditor with the assistance of the Fairlight management company and the fund administrator.

Market and Portfolio Performance

Fears relating to the U.S. tariff changes and what has so far turned out to be a V-shaped recovery dominated the first half of the quarter. Our portfolio followed the general pattern of the market, but our performance came from specific ideas, such as certain technology names and the junior gold miners. The perceived increased risk and uncertainty also appeared to push the price of gold from the $3,000/oz area to above $3,300/oz during the quarter.

Alongside this came the potential for returns from AI and AI-adjacent names, like the infrastructure businesses. We saw many investors take note, and took increasing notice ourselves, of the increasing flow of investment into the construction of AI data centers. Businesses that supply computing power and energy to these data centers had already risen a lot, but some names remained cheap, such as those supplying pipes, cooling systems, and concrete structures for these buildings. Several of these names have benefitted from increases in general infrastructure investment.

Overall in the quarter the S&P 500 index gained by 10.6% (excluding dividends). The index has now reached an earnings valuation multiple of 27.6 2. This is high relative to historical averages. From the Second World War to the 1990s the PE typically varied in the range of 10-20x, increasing subsequently, and then spiking in the Global Financial Crisis as the E in the ratio fell precipitously. The ratio has generally been increasing since then, excluding the Covid period when it spiked and fell back.

In addition, companies in high-growth sectors often report artificially low earnings because R&D and sales and marketing, which are really investments in future growth, are expensed immediately. As a result, the S&P 500 today contains more firms today where reported profits are structurally suppressed relative to underlying cash generation, helping to explain why headline P/E ratios appear higher than historical norms.

On the other hand, one could argue that higher PEs still reflect genuine risk: not all R&D and sales spending delivers returns, and some companies may be investing aggressively without clear paths to profitability. From this perspective, today’s valuations embed not only structural accounting effects but also optimistic assumptions about the long-term success of growth businesses. Ultimately earnings are an estimate, so it would be an interesting exercise to calculate an owner earnings PE figure for the S&P 500.

We had several drivers for returns in the quarter, and although our returns were not that dissimilar to the overall market, the factors for these returns were very different. We gained through the junior mining stocks, from a combination of improved results, increases in the gold price, and some re- rating. During the period we refocused on new idea generation to create a richer set of ideas to consider, and in particular we arrived at several infrastructure and AI-adjacent names. For example, TSS, Inc. (TSSI) had a very attractive valuation earlier in the quarter.

In late April we wrote a post about TSS, Inc. when it was trading at $7.53, giving it a market value of $189 million. Revenues had grown 172% from 2023 to 2024, driven by a 264% year-on-year increase in the systems integration segment through Q4 2024. Assuming more moderate growth and similar margins, the stock was trading at 12–13x forward earnings, or in the high single digits under more aggressive assumptions. The name re-rated within weeks, helped by shifting sentiment during the tariff relief rally, and further accelerated on May 15 when Q1 results showed revenues of $99 million, up 523% from the prior-year quarter.

The stock re-rated to above $30 by late June, pushing the valuation above $750 million. This was a difficult situation to assess with a rapidly growing company. On the one hand, growth had been exceptional; on the other, such momentum rarely lasts indefinitely, and we believed it was only a matter of time before it slowed. At that point, the earnings-based valuation appeared far less attractive. We therefore sold the position but continued to monitor the name along with several other AI infrastructure stocks, particularly those with multiple business segments that can support sales growth over a long horizon.

As of the time of writing, TSS, Inc. has fallen to the $17 area, partly due to results that disappointed the market and partly due to a public offering of common stock priced at $17 per share. Q2 revenues of $44.0 million were lower than Q1, but still represented a 262% year-on-year increase. Revenues remain lumpy at this stage in the company’s life cycle, but the overall growth has been extraordinary. We will continue to look past short-term noise around these kinds of stocks and continually compare the price presented by the market to our assessment of the fundamental value of the business.

Offshore

We have built up several positions and are investigating more in what might be called the “offshore trade”. This is a diverse set of businesses and sub-sectors tied to activity on the seas and oceans covering 71% of the earth’s surface. Valuations appear very low across many of these names in differing business areas, in spite of robust medium-term demand drivers that are producing high growth. These include the expansion of military and defense capabilities at sea, the growing need for marine energy production and related infrastructure, and the continued build-out of telecoms and data connectivity. This is a fertile ground for mispriced securities, although the theme has presented itself to us in reverse as we found an increasing number of cheap, growing names in the same area through a variety of searches. We will explore this theme, the specific companies we believe are best positioned to perform, and some specific ideas in more detail in future letters.

Elsewhere in the portfolio, Logic Instrument continued to deliver strong operating results, supported by the benefits of recent M&A activity. In cases like this, it is often important to trust management’s guidance, provided they have a record of honesty and accuracy. Here, we have a company that is projecting revenue growth from just under €30 million last year to nearly €50 million this year.

While the company slightly missed guidance last year 3, even a shortfall this year, say to €44 million, would still represent a 57% increase in revenues. As we have written previously, Logic Instrument benefits from a collection of partial moats which together provide meaningful protection for the business and are driving growth.

The company is a subsidiary of Archos (OTC:ACOSF), run by Loïc Poirier, and there are increasing signs that the child is outgrowing the parent. In 2018, revenues at Archos were €63.4 million compared to €11.2 million at Logic Instrument; by 2024, Archos had declined to €31.4 million while Logic Instrument had grown to €27.4 million. If Logic achieves anything close to its sales target this year, it is likely to surpass the parent in terms of the size of its income statement.

Concerns around the parent’s history of dilution and lack of profitability are likely having a depressing knock-on effect on Logic Instrument’s valuation. Archos has little or no moat, competing in the highly saturated consumer mobile and tablet market dominated by Samsung, Apple, and others. By contrast, Logic Instrument operates in the much more niche rugged device offshoot of the device market, where its competitors include Getac, Durabook, MilDef, and Panasonic’s Toughbook line. Its moat stems from geography (a European base serving local defense and industrial markets), history (an experienced salesforce with long-standing relationships), and technical expertise (meeting demanding military and industrial specifications).

Not All Moats are Built Equally

Much has been written about moats; which companies have them, which don’t, the elements of their construction, and whether those moats are growing or shrinking. To us, the black-and-white distinctions that are sometimes drawn don’t capture the full picture. Every sustainable company has a moat of some kind: a durable reason why it continues to exist. Moats take different forms: some are vast, global, and compounding; others are small, local, and fragile. And there are also companies whose moats are shrinking rapidly, once-protected businesses where the advantage has eroded to the point of being vanishingly small 4.

Moat 1

An example of a small moat would be a local business that is successful within its local niche. Picture your local neighborhood, nearest village, town, area of suburbia, or city center. Is there a restaurant, shop, or service business that you know which is always full, has great service, fantastic food, friendly staff, and keeps you coming back over and over again? Others likely feel the same, and you may find yourself thinking,“I wish I owned this place!”

The place I am thinking of has a multi-decade history, is locally famous, and I can’t imagine it ever closing unless the owner themselves went bankrupt. Even in that case, I am certain the exact same business would transfer to a new owner and remain just as successful, in fact it would be hard to disrupt it enough to make it fail.

However, I doubt it would work in most towns, and it has never opened another branch. In business terms, it probably only grows a little over inflation, but the profits are likely very healthy. This kind of business has a moat, but a very specific, localized, and limited one. To attempt to compete with it and open a similar business next door, would be very foolish, and the recipe for success would not work elsewhere. But an owner could operate it for decades and live very well off the proceeds. Does it have a moat?

  • Brand → Ambience and design: the specific vibe and interior of the location.
  • Operational/Business → The restaurant works well across brunch, lunch, dinner and late evening, keeping it busy throughout the day and maximizing revenue relative to fixed costs (building, staff).
  • Brand/Product → Social energy and private hire: well known locally, it draws parties and has a cachet that would take years to replicate. Product → Good food: the core business requirement is met.

Moat 2

Now think of another business that operates as a local chain. Again, it could be a restaurant, retail, or service company. I’m thinking of one local to us. It has grown like a weed, does something special, started off in the local area and region, but now looks set to expand nationally. If you like cupcakes, Magnolia Bakery may come to mind, a business that started locally but is now recognized nationally. Its moat?

  • Brand → New York-style cupcakes, featured on Sex and the City.
  • Product → Simple to replicate at scale, with long shelf life, and seasonal editions.
  • Operational → Flagship stores and control across locations.

Moat 3

Finally, let’s think of a very famous, global business; highly successful, with a moat that seems obvious. But even here there are obstacles it cannot cross. As an example, let’s choose Apple, Inc. It is hard to argue with the company’s success, cash flow generation, growth, and uniqueness. In business terms, Apple is extraordinary, having grown through several phases to become one of the largest companies in the world, particularly in the second Steve Jobs era, from 1997 onwards until his passing.

The moat during this period?

  • Brand → One of the most valuable brands ever created. Design with emotional resonance, ecosystem and user experience (“it just works”), iconic marketing and storytelling.
  • Product → Seamless hardware-software integration, Apple ecosystem lock-in, and simplicity in form and function.
  • Operational/Business → Vertical integration and end-to-end-control, supply chain mastery and exclusivity, Apple Stores and the Genius Bar.

So how much moat does each of these companies really have? The restaurant has a fairly strong product and branding, but may only work in the local area, not being that well known outside of a small radius. It also suffers from the issue of only being able to provide service physically to customers locally, it’s unlikely to receive orders nationwide!

The second moat is stronger, with national recognition and some unusual product and brand features. But we can easily imagine competitors entering the space, food fashions changing, or the company making a misstep.

The final moat is the widest and, from 1997 onwards, was expanding rapidly. By 2010 Apple had built a massive national moat and had extended it globally. Yet even in this case it faced growing competition in Southeast Asia, although those competitors had to copy much of Apple’s technology, design ideas, and product advances to compete.

Returning to Logic Instrument, it may at first appear to have little or no moat. Its main moat lies in its the niche products, but even there it has competitors. However, if globalization reduces, and products — especially those linked to military application — become more regional, that could increase its moat. The company has also won several contracts that demonstrate, as the company itself has noted, that its salesforce is deeply embedded in certain markets and customer relationships. Also the company’s ISO certifications and exacting specifications of its rugged devices also create barriers to entry, since developing technologies that meet such requirements takes significant time and expertise. Taken together, these factors amount to a much larger moat than may be seen at first glance.

A comparison from the real world would be the moat of Bourtange in the Netherlands. Pictures of this fortress show a series of waterways and boggy ground partially preventing access. Although entry was technically possible for an attacking army, the fortress remained uncaptured for centuries after construction. Its complex series of partial moats made it essentially impossible to attack. Like Bourtange, which was never captured after its completion in 1593, Logic Instrument’s defense does not rest on a single towering wall but on a series of layered moats that together make its position far more resilient than it first appears.

Gold Call Options

Gold has now risen into the $3,400/oz area (as of the time of writing). Although the prices of junior gold miners have risen overall, they still appear to be lagging the implied valuation of their businesses. There is a wide margin of safety between the prices of these stocks, if one thinks of them as a series of quarterly call options struck at the AISC 5 for each business, and with the gold selling price as the price of the underlying. Gold December futures now stand at $3,404/oz and, at these levels, the businesses we own are still trading on an earnings multiple of 3-4x; cheap, especially given the expected growth in production.

Last summer, when we began to notice the junior gold miners, the price of gold was around $2,500/oz. An example miner had an AISC around $1,900/oz, and set to decrease costs to $1,800/oz. That implied a selling margin of $600/oz, and the company was producing 10,000oz per quarter.

This equated to an operating profit of $6 million per quarter, or $24 million per year. At the time it was value at $75 million and looked set to have cash of $20 million by the end of the year. Cheap on the face of it, or conversely, showing a large margin of safety against a fall in the gold price.

This same business is now producing 11,000oz per quarter, likely rising to 12,000oz before year-end. At a gold selling price of $3,200/oz, with no AISC improvement, the margin would be $1,300/oz, implying annual operating profit of $62.4 million. It’s now valued at $244 million and has net cash of $25 million, likely increasing well above $30 million by year-end. On an enterprise valuation of $214 million, it still trades at 3.4x; a great business even after the share price has risen has risen 220%. To answer the question the reader may be asking at this point: Serabi Gold plc (OTCQX:SRBIF).

But it’s not the only one. There are many other similar stocks in the gold and broader precious metals space, with a common theme of underpricing relative to current gold levels and sustaining costs, alongside growth opportunities from exploration.

Always Be Searching

We are continuing with our regular Substack posts, where we will take deeper dives into new names we are researching and markets that we think are interesting. The harder we look, the more opportunities we find. We will keep searching as hard as we can, across some of the cheapest and fastest-growing markets, sectors, and companies in the world.
