data.title: RSF: Managed 10% Distribution Yield And Unique Interval Structure
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

RiverNorth Capital and Income Fund (RSF) is a rather unique closed-end fund, as it is an interval fund that is listed publicly for investors to buy and sell. An interval structure repurchases shares quarterly, between 5-25%. With this feature, an investor can receive a small portion of the net asset value of their holding regularly. That means any discount that the fund trades at is somewhat attractive. However, the fund does overpay on its distribution relative to what it has been able to earn, and that means that NAV has continually trended lower.

When a CEF is structured this way, they are almost entirely non-listed. So having the ability to buy and sell throughout the trading day also adds further liquidity for investors, outside of simply the repurchases. For non-listed interval funds, the only liquidity is that quarterly liquidation of a fraction of their position or a distribution.

RSF Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 1.67
  • Discount/Premium: -4.98%
  • Distribution Yield: 11.04%
  • Expense Ratio: 3.02%
  • Leverage: 0%
  • Managed Assets: $65.8 million
  • Structure: Interval CEF

RSF's investment objective is "a high level of current income." They attempt to achieve this through "investing in credit instruments, including a portfolio of securities of specialty finance and other Financial companies that the Fund's Advisor, RiverNorth Capital Management, LLC believes offer attractive opportunities for income." They have no restrictions on maturity or credit quality.

Leverage and Portfolio Shift

This is an incredibly small fund as well, with the fund's total net assets comes in at just $65.8 million. In our last update, they were leveraged, but they've taken that down to zero as of the end of June 30, 2025.

This isn't necessarily a negative, as they were highly leveraged previously at over 42%. The leverage they were using was a fixed-rate 5.875% Series A Term Preferred, but that had a mandatory redemption on October 31, 2024.

On a better note, the fund was able to raise leverage by issuing preferreds that pay a fixed dividend. These are the RiverNorth 5.875% Series A Term Preferred (RMPL.PR). Now, back to more bad news: these come with a mandatory redemption that is coming up quite quickly on October 31, 2024. These were originally issued in 2017 when rates were much lower than they are now. It will be interesting to see if the fund pursues another preferred issuance and at what rate they may have to offer.

The fund is overwhelmingly invested in small business whole loans.

More specifically, it is through Square with a rather wide range of rates and maturities. Though they are generally quite short-term, with the longest maturity only going to mid-2026.

I mention this after they deleveraged through redeeming their outstanding preferred, because previously, the fund was invested quite differently. The largest allocation was to the small business loans. Still, they also held more material allocations to investment company debt, other CEFs, and a bit more allocated to SPACs as well. So while they have quite a bit of flexibility in where they can invest, they've seemed to find their niche in this category.

Additionally, with the investments, they appear to pay a rather high total expense ratio due to loan service fees. When including that and leverage (when they had used leverage), it pushes the fund's total expense ratio to 7.94%.

Rights Offering and Discount Appeal

The fund recently wrapped up a rights offering in July, and that was a dilutive offering since it was done at a discount. The formula hit the floor of the offering at 90% of NAV (i.e., a 10% discount). That further chipped away at the NAV—when it had already been trending mostly lower anyway.

West Palm Beach, FL– July 9, 2025 – RiverNorth Capital and Income Fund, Inc. (NYSE: RSF) (the “Fund”) today announced the final results of its transferable rights offering (the “Offering”). The Fund will issue a total of 1,105,000 new shares of common stock as a result of the Offering, which closed on July 7, 2025 (the “Expiration Date”).

The subscription price of $14.39 per share in the Offering was established on the Expiration Date based on a formula equal to 90% of the reported net asset value per share of common stock. Gross proceeds received by the Fund, before any expenses of the Offering, are expected to total approximately $15.9 million.

This was the third year in a row that they've conducted a rights offering. In 2023, it saw a $0.48 hit to NAV, and 2024 saw a $0.43 NAV hit. This year's was similar, at around what would have been a $0.44 clipping of the NAV per share.

As we noted previously, any discount on this fund can be appealing due to the quarterly redemptions that are available to investors. That allows investors to receive a small portion of the NAV back, thus realizing the discount. Similar to all other tender offers, not all shareholders will participate. Sometimes it simply isn't attractive to participate if the fund is trading at NAV already or at a premium.

However, the fund has more regularly been trading at a discount for most of its life—meaning that most often it is in an investor's best interest to participate. That said, investors who do participate would be hoping that other investors don't, so that a larger slice of their shares would be accepted.

Now, the fund does pay out an attractively high rate as it is a managed 10% NAV that resets annually. The problem is that the fund hasn't been able to sustain that rate and keep NAV up at the same time. This is even as the fund's net investment income has been able to cover its distribution. It is the underlying losses in the portfolio and dilutive rights offerings that negatively impact the fund.

Therefore, the discount has appeal when it can be realized, but one should expect that before too long, the NAV price being accepted will be lower than your cost basis on picking up shares.

In other words, you would eventually expect to see a capital loss.

That's probably a hard sell for most investors. Interestingly, though, Sit Investments and Karpus Management, activists in the CEF space, hold rather sizeable positions in the fund.

Karpus had been selling out of the fund, but they still are listed as owning 26%. Sit Investment has been purchasing more recently and is sitting at nearly a 12% stake in the fund.

They could be looking to potentially push the fund to liquidate, or hope that the fund would liquidate on its own. This is because RiverNorth themselves are a bit like Sit Investment, where they aren't necessarily aggressive activists—they are more like accomplices. Where Saba Capital and Karpus are actively putting capital to work and being aggressive, Sit and RiverNorth will often be in those same funds.

On the other hand, despite the NAV erosion and high expense ratio, the fund still puts up what is relatively attractive total returns. Since the fund's inception, during Covid, it was a massive laggard. However, coming out of Covid, they were a very strong performer—presumably due to leverage. Since then, it has been able to outpace in terms of total returns on both a NAV and share price basis compared to fixed-income alternatives.

That would include both the investment-grade and below-investment-grade corporate bond categories as measured by ETFs of iShares iBoxx Investment Grade Corporate Bond (LQD) and iShares iBoxx High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG). Further, it has outperformed the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) as well, from another fixed-income angle.

The fund itself puts its performance up against the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index—which it also pretty easily outperforms on most measurement periods.

Worth noting is that even when the fund held a material sleeve of CEFs, they were largely fixed-income focused. There was some exposure to equities, but it was a relatively small portion of its portfolio. Meaning that comparing the fund to fixed-income-focused measuring sticks feels appropriate.

Conclusion

RSF is a truly unique fund with being listed publicly as an interval structured CEF. Further, with its investment focus, there isn't another fund that comes to mind providing this exact exposure.

Unique doesn't necessarily make it good, though. It is an incredibly small fund that has quarterly redemptions and overpays a distribution, which has continually seen the fund grow smaller and smaller. Unlike its non-listed counterparts, new capital doesn't flow in the same way.

Instead, they conduct rights offerings. That brings in new capital but is done at a discount and results in a further dilutive hit to the NAV. The fund also has quite a high expense ratio. All things that help contribute to a lower trending NAV per share over time make it look unappealing for most investors.

On the other hand, despite simply looking at a price chart, the fund's total returns relative to other fixed-income instruments are quite respectable, as they've outperformed. Activist investors have seemingly taken notice as well, and they hold a sizeable overall allocation of the fund—albeit with such a small fund, it isn't hard to build up what looks like a massive stake for these firms.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cenovus Energy: Thanks For Paying In Cash
data.description:
Cenovus Energy (NYSE:CVE) has announced a 5.2 billion USD (7.9 billion CAD) deal to acquire MEG Energy (OTCPK:MEGEF), as the almost $30 billion integrated energy company undergoes expansion once more. The two companies have heavily complementary assets, and as we'll see in this article, the 75% cash acquisition is an intelligent and profitable deal.

For reference at this time, 1 CAD ~ 0.72 USD.

Transaction Overview

Cenovus Energy is grabbing 110 thousand barrels/day of low-cost production and assets that integrate tightly into its existing footprint.

This enables Cenovus Energy to substantially expand its Christina Lake assets getting access to wells on the boundary of prior shared leases (100m previous regulatory setback) along with synergies from the shared operations. Cenovus expects within the next few years to unlock more than $400 million/year in synergies.

Cenovus Energy expects this asset to be immediately accretive downstream despite the heavy cash component with a <$45 WTI supply cost and it plans to maintain strong investment-grade credit ratings.

The transaction will be roughly 75% cash and 25% stock for the actual acquisition along with 0.9 billion CAD in assumed net debt and liabilities. CVE has the financials to make it happen and expects to close the acquisition by year-end. The ~$55K/barrel/day of 2028 production is ~$15/barrel of annual production, a great rate for a <$45 WTI breakeven.

Cenovus Energy ends with an incredibly manageable balance sheet and investment grade credit rating.

Transaction Asset Base

The strong combined asset base is visible below, along with Cenovus Energy's prior Foster Creek acquisition.

Cenovus Energy provides a massive chunk of fully contiguous assets and brings together some of the most efficient steam oil sands projects in the industry. Cenovus also acquires a number of other assets with a massive ~5.6 billion barrels in place, showing their long life and export capacity (a major issue in Canada) to cover most of production.

Cenovus Energy expects ~$150 million of near-term 2026 synergies, which should come quite quickly as the overhead operations of both companies are merged. There's also a ~$200 million one-time benefit and a number of 2028~ continued enhancements from Cenovus Energy's improved integrated asset portfolio.

An example of the improvement opportunities is visible above, where Cenovus Energy can take advantage of contiguous assets to create wells that are further apart and much longer. This is all through a single surface location and less well pairs, which help it to keep costs much lower and capture the same resource.

Financial Outlook

CVE's financial outlook is for strong shareholder returns as it cleans up its balance sheet as a result.

Cenovus Energy has a 3-year term loan that it plans to pay off as it comes due to hit its debt targets. Keeping the debt short-term but paying it off helps with interest expenditures. There's also a debt bridge that it'll use to issue longer-term debt to manage its balance sheet.

The company can continue to fund its 3.6% dividend yield and sustain capital at a mere $45/barrel WTI. That's almost $20/barrel below current WTI prices. The long-duration nature of oil sands means that the company can continue its production and shareholder returns without needing to constantly explore the same as for example shale oil firms.

It's worth noting short-term Cenovus Energy has higher capital obligations due to the White Rose development (50% of growth capital) that will be completed soon. Total sustaining capital outside of growth comes in at $2.3 billion USD annually.

CVE has a consistent history of growing its dividend at double-digits for several years now while executing its buyback program, and we'd like to see it continue executing that successfully. The cash-based acquisition saves it on offsetting its buyback program, and shows commitment to its policy of shareholder returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is WTI prices. Cenovus Energy can sustain its base dividend at $45/barrel, but prices have dropped below that before. The merged company is purely in the oil sands business, which already tends to trade at a discount to less takeaway capacity, and weak oil prices could hurt its ability to justify the acquisition and drive returns.

Conclusion

Cenovus Energy has recently announced a plan for a multi-billion dollar acquisition of MEG Energy, expected to close by year-end. The acquisition will provide hundreds of millions of dollars in synergies, both short- and long-term, and enable the company to improve operations due to removing shared lease overlap and new drilling strategies.

Cenovus's balance sheet has more than enough capacity to afford the acquisition and maintain shareholder returns. We expect the company to continue its policy of share repurchases and dividends, and the non-dilutive acquisition helps with those goals. Overall, that makes MEG Energy a valuable acquisition.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ACV: Tax-Efficient Income CEF With Growth Potential
data.description:
Overview

Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund (NYSE:ACV) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns from its blended portfolio of assets. The fund utilizes income producing securities alongside traditional equities, which provides investors with the opportunity to get the best of both worlds: income generation and capital appreciation. Blended funds like ACV can be a great way to hedge against uncertainty while markets hover near their all time highs.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that ACV's share price has increased by about 6.5%. The fund was capable of riding the positive market momentum over recent months, which helped it recover from the decline earlier in the year. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 18.2% over the same time frame. ACV offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 9.6%, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a high-yield. Although the latest annual report indicates that the fund can generate a sufficient level of earnings, the distribution coverage has been inconsistent over the last few years.

I think the fund currently trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation, which makes this an interesting time to begin accumulating shares. I like the underlying mix of assets and I believe the fund can remain a defensive choice during continued market volatility. Additionally, future interest rate cuts can likely serve as a growth catalyst. A lower cost of debt can improve the fund's operating margins and provide relief for existing income securities. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategies that ACV implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's latest overview, ACV has total managed assets of $349.7M that are spread across a blended portfolio of income producing securities. The fund aims to invest at least 50% of its assets into convertible securities. As the name implies, 'convertible' securities are hybrid instruments that are structured as bonds and generate income for the fund. However, they are unique because management has the right to convert these income securities into common shares, if they believe that the underlying position has the ability to provide a meaningful level of growth. The breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Convertible Securities: 57.75%
  • Common Equities: 22.43%
  • High Yield Bonds: 16.26%
  • Cash & Equivalents: 3.61%

As a way to expand on its diversity and reduce risk, ACV spreads its assets across a wide range of sectors. As we can see below, ACV is most heavily allocated to assets in the Information Technology sector at 28.79% of the portfolio. This is followed by exposure to Financials and Industrials investments, which accounts for 13.34% and 12.54% of assets respectively. The combination of these top three sectors make up over 54% of ACV's total assets, so investors should anticipate that these sectors have the greatest impact on the fund's daily price change and total return potential.

Looking at the top holdings below, we can see many notable large-cap names. For instance, the largest holding is currently Nvidia (NVDA) at 3.01% of assets. This is followed by exposure to several other large-cap companies such as the Boeing Company (BA), Microsoft (MSFT), Wells Fargo (WFC), Welltower (WELL), and Amazon (AMZN) just to name a few. However, we should note that some of these positions are held through convertible securities, so ACV's ability to participate in the full price movement of these underlying assets may be limited.

Just to demonstrate the fund's ability to execute its blended approach, I pulled in a performance chart of ACV over the last ten years. As we can see below, ACV's share price has only increased by about 18.45% over the last decade. When including all distributions, the total return jumps up to 259.2% over the same period. Therefore, investors should understand that ACV is best utilized for the income that can be received. If you're an investor looking to maximize total return or capture the growth of the market, ACV may not be the best choice for you.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since ACV is structured as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, ACV currently trades at a small discount to NAV of approximately 4.86%. For reference, ACV traded at an average discount to NAV of 2.41% over the last three year period, which can be an indication that it is now a good time for accumulation of shares. To better conceptualize the discount level in relation to its ten year range, I added the red line in the graph below.

To get better clarity into the internal performance of the fund, I reviewed the latest annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.15 per share, while net realized gains were much stronger at $4.47 per share. Looking at prior years, net investment income levels were much lower due to the fluctuating interest rate environment. For a fund that puts emphasis on income generation, I would like to see net investment income levels substantially improved at some point in the future.

Since net investment income levels are relatively low, this leaves ACV reliant on net realized gains to fuel growth. The only issue is that net realized gains are dependent on positive market conditions so that ACV can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Therefore, the fund offers very little resilience through market declines, which is why we can see that ACV experienced a large net realized loss of $3.65 per share in 2023, following the market decline throughout 2022. As a result of the inconsistent performance in recent years, ACV has struggled to regain positive NAV growth. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $35.15 per share
  • 2022: $27.25 per share
  • 2023: $21.22 per share
  • 2024: $20.85 per share
  • 2025: $23.31 per share

As part of the fund's operating strategy, ACV utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify earnings when an attractive opportunity is available. Total effective leverage currently amounts to 30.02% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $75M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage in this higher interest rate environment. Investors must consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated to investments that do not perform as anticipated.

The challenge is that the higher interest rate environment makes it more costly for the fund to utilize leverage. Therefore, the fund's operating spread between the cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities are thinner, which makes it more difficult to expand earnings. This is why ACV has demonstrated such a vulnerability to the interest rate environment. Looking at the chart below, we can see how ACV has shared an inverse relationship to the federal funds rate. When interest rates were cut to near zero levels, ACV's share price rapidly increased throughout 2020 and 2021. Conversely, ACV's share price retracted from its highs and remained suppressed after interest rates were hiked throughout 2022 and 2023.

Therefore, I anticipate that future interest rate cuts will serve as a catalyst for growth. The market seems to be anticipating interest rate cuts to happen as soon as September, due to core CPI data coming in slightly hot. Lower interest rates would improve the fund's operating spread and stimulate the growth of underlying equities. Therefore, I believe that accumulation here would be ideal for long-term investors.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.18 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.6%. If we multiply this monthly distribution amount by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $2.16 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, ACV has an inconsistent history of being able to generate a sufficient level of income to support these distributions. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $12.72 per share
  • 2022: ($0.08) per share
  • 2023: ($3.67) per share
  • 2024: $1.79 per share
  • 2025: $4.62 per share

However, ACV has experienced stronger earnings years where the income collected is capable of supporting distributions for multiple years. For instance, 2025's annual earnings of $4.62 per share is sufficient enough to cover more than two full years of payouts. This sort of sporadic earnings can be difficult to estimate but it has been efficient at allowing ACV to keep payouts consistent over the last few years. Therefore, I am not concerned about the fund reducing payouts in the near-term, even in a scenario where interest rates are reduced.

The most important aspect of the distribution is the tax-efficiency that the fund can provide. According to the latest section19(a) notice, ACV's distributions are mostly comprised of return of capital distributions, amounting to 74.8% of distributions YTD. Return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Return of capital distributions reduce an investor's cost basis over time, which allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

Therefore, ACV is a tax-efficient holding that is a good fit for income investors that want to limit their tax liability. However, tax-efficient doesn't necessarily equate to tax-free. As we can see, a little more than a tenth of the distributions YTD are funded by net investment income and short-term capital gains, which are both classified as ordinary income and have the highest tax burden for investors.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate ACV as a buy due to the attractive valuation and the potential growth catalyst of future interest rates. The fund's share price has remained suppressed as interest rates remain near their decade high. A higher interest rate has made it more difficult for NAV growth to occur, since the fund's operating spread is smaller. Future interest rate cuts can improve the operating spread and lead to healthier earnings and NAV growth over time. The fund's blended portfolio of assets also provides investors with the opportunity to experience capital appreciation over time. Lastly, the tax-efficient nature of the distributions makes it an attractive choice for income investors that want to limit their tax liability.

data.title: 2 REITs To Buy Before September 17th
data.description:
The Fed Chair, Jerome Powell, just had his highly anticipated speech at the Federal Reserve's annual symposium at Jackson Hole, and his comments seemed to strongly suggest that interest rates would soon be cut to lower levels. Here are the statements that I am referring to:

"With policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance..."

"Risks to inflation are tilted to the upside, and risks to employment to the downside—a challenging situation,"

He makes it clear that interest rates are restrictive to the economy, which is now weakening at least in part because of the trade war.

Therefore, he is opening the door for a near-term "adjustment in their policy," which clearly signals that they expect to cut interest rates in the near term.

It was telegraphed so clearly that the debt market is now pricing a 75% chance of a rate cut at the next meeting on September 17th:

Not only that, but interest rates are expected to keep getting cut further over the next year. The current expectation is that interest rates will be cut a total of five times within a year from now:

This would be very bullish for REITs, which have suffered a 3-year-long bear market due to the high-interest rates.

It is so bullish that REITs (VNQ) surged by 2%, the most in a while, on the day of these remarks:

Rate cuts benefit REITs in two main ways:

  • It reduces yields on money market funds and short-term treasuries, which then pushes investors to look for alternatives to earn higher yields. Historically, a lot of that capital has flown to the REIT sector when interest rates were low, leading to higher REIT valuations.
  • It increases their profitability by reducing their interest expense. Short-term rates also influence long-term rates, which dropped significantly as well on the day of the Fed's latest remarks.

So in short:

Higher multiples + higher earnings = significant upside

But what REITs are today offering the most upside potential?

It is those who stand to benefit the most because of their heavy leverage and low valuations. Many such REITs are down 50% or more in this bear market and could more than double just to reach previous peaks.

In what follows, I am going to highlight two such examples that we currently hold in our Portfolio at High Yield Landlord.

Clipper Realty (CLPR)

Clipper surged by 4.2% on the day of the Fed's remarks, more than the rest of the REIT sector:

And this is really just the tip of the iceberg.

The REIT has seen its share price drop from about $13 right before the pandemic to just $4 today:

The main reason for this sharp drop is its heavy leverage. The REIT has a 16x debt-to-EBITDA, which puts it at great risk if interest rates were to remain higher for longer.

It would gradually lose its profitability as it is forced to refinance debt at higher interest rates, or worse, it may eventually fail to refinance and face bankruptcy.

But its properties are actually performing well. The REIT is primarily invested in affordable apartment communities in Brooklyn and Manhattan, which are today enjoying rapid rent growth. In the last quarter, they achieved record revenues after rising another 5%.

Their CEO made the following note on their conference call:

"Overall rents are generally at all-time highs and continuing to increase, and we are nearly fully leased. In the second quarter, new leases exceeded prior rents by over 14% across the entire portfolio, as I will further detail."

This signaled that the solid rent growth is set to continue.

Therefore, despite its crashing share price, the value of its assets hasn't dropped nearly that much.

We estimate its net asset value per share at about $8-9 per share, which is roughly double its current share price.

This means that if we now get lower interest rates and you remove the concern over its balance sheet, its stock could strongly recover. It could surge by 50% and it would still be a long way from reaching its net asset value.

While you wait, you also earn a near 10% dividend yield, which the market clearly views as unsustainable, but if rates are cut enough, it may surprise the market by maintaining its dividend, which would then attract a lot of yield-oriented investors.

That makes it a great opportunity if you have a higher tolerance for risk and expect significant rate cuts in the near term.

The recent surge in its share price could be just the beginning.

Medical Properties Trust (MPW)

It has been a while since we covered MPW.

The REIT has been a battleground stock for the past few years and for good reasons. It became the target of short sellers who accused the REIT of not being transparent about its biggest tenant's financial issues. In hindsight, they proved to be correct, as that tenant later filed for bankruptcy, and the REIT crashed to much lower levels.

Since then, those properties have been released to other tenants, which is great news for investors, but the stock still hasn't recovered all that much as it stained the company's reputation, and fears persist about the REIT's ability to handle its debt.

Now, the rent payments of these new tenants are gradually ramping up, and the management appears very confident that their rental income will rise significantly going into 2026. Here is what their CEO noted:

"With steady contributions from our stabilized portfolio and a rapidly ramping portfolio of new operators, we remain confident in our ability to reach total annualized cash rent of more than $1 billion by year-end 2026."

"Beginning in October 2026, we expect to be collecting 100% of fully ramped rent totaling about $160 million on an annualized basis."

If by then, interest rates have also been cut significantly, as the market is predicting, then MPW could surge to a lot higher levels.

Just three years ago, it still traded at over $20.

Today, it trades at just $4 due to tenant and leverage concerns.

If it can put those to rest over the next year, I think that it could easily surge 50% or more from here. While you wait, it is also paying you a 7.4% dividend yield.

Again, this is a riskier pick, but if you have the risk tolerance and expect lower interest rates, it could be a great choice.

Closing Note

At High Yield Landlord, we manage two portfolios. One that seeks to maximize total returns at the expense of higher risk, and this portfolio holds numerous speculative names like CLPR and MPW. Our other portfolio follows a more conservative strategy and seeks to maximize safe income, avoiding this type of REIT. Ultimately, you need to decide what you are looking to achieve and invest accordingly.

data.title: Triumph's True Value Is Hidden
data.description:
Triumph Financial (TFIN) is a regional bank and a trucking workflows technology provider mashed into one company. While its banking and factoring operations are mature and solidly profitable, the technology offerings it has grown into recently are still in their infancy. The recent surge in expenses required to stand up Triumph's new offerings combined with a freight recession have had a depressing effect on the company's share price. However, a qualitative look at the company's unique position within the trucking industry reveals significant value that should manifest itself through strong financial results eventually.

Business Model

The trucking industry is as fragmented as it is large. The market collects $875 billion each year in freight revenue, but the top 15 full truckload [FTL] carriers combined account for just 10% of the market. Over 95% of carriers operate 10 trucks or less. This fragmentation presents certain challenges for each player.

The companies shipping products (“shippers”) must ultimately pay hundreds of different trucking companies each year, keep track of shipments, monitor carrier insurance and compliance, and prevent increasingly prevalent fraud. To centralize some of these functions, shippers typically turn to “brokers” who serve as a single point of contact.

Brokers, however, must solve the challenges they take on from the shippers, plus a few more. Though brokers are a bit less fragmented than trucking companies, there are still around 26,000 operating in America. They must balance the shipper’s desire to pay on a net-30 to 60-day basis with the trucker’s wish to be paid soon after completing a shipment. Brokers also face a gargantuan amount of back-office work auditing invoices, sending payments to carriers, and collecting payments from shippers.

Truckers (known as “carriers”) must contend with payment delays and take on counterparty risk from brokers. Carriers often face late payments from clients – average days sales outstanding amount to 41 days, and average terms are typically 30 days or more. Carriers also face the risk of getting nothing at all if one of the brokers or shippers they’ve accepted a job through goes out of business.

These entrenched problems create the need for Triumph’s offerings, which are organized in four segments – Banking, Factoring, Payments, and Intelligence.

Banking

While much of this article focuses on Triumph’s role within the trucking industry, its largest segment, which serves as the company’s foundation, is a fairly conventional regional bank. TBK Bank offers traditional banking services, with branches sprinkled around the Midwest. Triumph’s CEO, Aaron Graft, stated in his 2022 annual report letter that he wanted the bank to consistently achieve 20% ROE and 2% ROA – numbers which would put them amongst the most capital-efficient banks in the country. Triumph doesn’t break out bank-specific shareholders' equity, but the segment did earn 1.6% and 2.1% on assets in 2024 and 2023, respectively. This is a fantastic result considering ROA above 1% is generally considered “good” and above 2% is "excellent." Though these numbers are impressive, they actually understate the bank’s profitability because roughly 25% of their loan book is lent to their factoring and payments segments at Federal Home Loan Bank advance rates, which are currently around 4%. This is wholly advantageous for Triumph’s business and underlies the success of their other segments, but the below-market lending rate also leads to a reduction in interest that would otherwise be allocated to the bank.

Outside of intercompany loans, the largest industries represented on the bank’s loan books are mortgage warehousing, commercial real estate, and commercial equipment. These loans are all relatively short-term in nature, which I find attractive during the current period of fiscal profligacy. Crucially, they also had enough foresight to avoid the massive unrealized losses on long-term bonds resulting from the quick rise in interest rates in 2022, incurring just $8 million in unrealized losses that year net of taxes.

The banking segment provides steady earnings, which serve as a hedge against the more volatile earnings it gets from segments related to the highly cyclical trucking market. More importantly, the bank provides:

  1. A source of low-cost funding for its factoring operations
  2. Existing payment rails on which to provide financial services to companies in the trucking industry
  3. The compliance infrastructure to handle significant regulatory requirements in their factoring and payments businesses
  4. Trust: parties on both the sending and receiving ends of payments know they can trust Triumph because the transactions are executed through compliant banking operations.

TBK Bank is the strategic backbone supporting the company’s factoring and payments businesses. In my description of those segments below, I describe how this structure uniquely positions the company against its competitors.

Factoring

Small to midsize carriers often turn to factoring to solve the issue of delayed payments from shippers. Providers of factoring services, known as "factors," purchase carrier receivables at a slight discount (typically between 1% and 5%) and deal with collecting those invoices themselves. Carriers get immediate capital to fund their businesses and remove the back-office work involved with chasing down payments. Factors get a healthy return on these purchases – Triumph had earned around 17% on factor purchases before the pandemic, though that number has since come down to the low teens.

While it can be very profitable, factoring—like trucking—is a very fragmented business with low barriers to entry. Still, Triumph has spent several years and a significant amount of capex building out their tech stack in an effort to improve the efficiency of processing thousands of carrier invoices, giving them a slight edge over smaller competitors. Early this year, the company actually launched a new Factoring as a Service [FAAS] offering sharing this tech stack with their competitors. While this move seems counterproductive at first (and was met with initial skepticism by the factoring industry), the reasoning is sound. While Triumph has maintained a 15% market share in the factoring industry for several years, offering this back-office tech platform will give them a way to generate attractive recurring fee revenues while lessening their reliance on the highly cyclical and competitive factoring industry.

Triumph is the second largest factor in the industry, and its competitive advantages come both from its tech stack and its low cost of funding. Low-cost bank funding gives Triumph the flexibility to either reduce rates to gain market share or collect a larger spread. The trust inspired by their compliant banking operations was likely a factor in the “Factoring powered by Delta” arrangement with C.H. Robinson. America’s largest broker would be unlikely to launch a factoring product with any companies that lack the financial backing or compliance functions of Triumph. And there are no other companies in the industry that operate as a bank, factor, and payments provider to the trucking industry.

The Factoring segment generated about $110 million in revenues in 2024 – good for 26% of the company’s total. This number was above $200 million during the heady trucking days of 2021-2022, but the recent “freight recession” has reduced the number of receivables available for purchase. Still, the segment is operating profitably with a 13.75% yield on average receivable balance in 2024 and an operating margin of 19%.

Payments

Triumph’s move into back-end factoring software services seems to be following a similar path to that taken in their “Payments” segment. The company originally offered their “TriumphPay” platform to Brokers and Shippers in an effort to make their main factoring product more attractive. However, after acquiring a workflow solutions software company named “HubTran” in 2021, Triumph pivoted towards providing payment services to brokers and factors. These services include:

  1. Payments: Brokers use TriumphPay to send payments to carriers (or the factor if a carrier uses one). These payments are fulfilled at a low cost through TBK bank through ACH, wire, check, or directly to a LoadPay account (discussed below).
  2. Accounts payable automation: Brokers use TriumphPay’s automated invoice approval processing (capabilities acquired through HubTran) to simplify A/P functions and detect fraud. Fees are paid on a per-invoice basis.
  3. QuickPay: When carriers opt for faster payment, TriumphPay charges a percentage-based fee, which depends on whether Triumph or the broker front the money.
  4. LoadPay: Launched in 2024, LoadPay is a bank account for carriers that can receive payments faster through Triumph’s own banking network. These accounts have debit cards, which also generate interchange fees.
  5. Supply chain finance: Triumph fronts broker payments to carriers and charges an interest spread based on SOFR.
  6. Intersegment interest on float: Triumph holds capital on a short-term basis for its customers. In aggregate, the interest from this “float” can be material.

TriumphPay offers significant value to brokers by measurably reducing workload and saving money. By using the TriumphPay tech stack, brokers are able to use machine learning tools to automate invoice data extraction and approval while cutting down on fraud. This centralized platform also structures data on thousands of individual shipments and provides reporting functions for these atomized transactions.

The TriumphPay platform charges many of its fees on a per-invoice basis, making it easily scalable no matter the size of their broker clients. And while it’s easy to see how centralizing and automating back-office functions would be advantageous for smaller brokers without a large workforce, it’s telling that TriumphPay was adopted by C.H. Robinson and ArcBest – the largest and 12th largest brokers in America, respectively.

Triumph’s banking operations enhance the competitiveness of this segment by reducing both the cost and time required to execute payments. In fact, for payments sent from a broker on TriumphPay to a carrier on LoadPay, payments happen instantly and at no cost to the carrier because they’re processed internally at TBK Bank. And again, Triumph’s customers feel a level of trust with their funds stored at an FDIC-insured bank that competitors can’t replicate.

Triumph didn’t start breaking out data specific to the Payments segment until after its 2021 HubTran acquisition. This is still a growing business, which ultimately generates a net loss for the company, though the magnitude of that loss has declined from $27 million in 2022 to $12 million in 2024. Revenues have been growing at a 24% CAGR over that period, from $36 million to $55 million.

Intelligence

With their growing payments and factoring services, Triumph now touches over 50% of brokered freight transactions in America. These transactions generate a valuable trove of data for the company, which caused them to launch a new “Intelligence” segment at the beginning of 2025. This segment came into focus through two notable acquisitions: GreenScreens.ai (acquired in May 2025), and Isometric Technologies (acquired in December 2024). These acquisitions will help Triumph provide two primary data services through this new segment:

  1. Triumph Rate Intelligence: Leveraging TriumphPay’s data and the GreenScreens.ai platform, the company will offer predictive and customized pricing recommendations based on verified transaction data. Most products that inform pricing data today are based on market averages and older data. This new product is uniquely placed to give logistics firms a sharper tool to quote spot loads and contracts.
  2. Triumph Performance: This service is based on the ISO platform and focuses on freight service quality analytics. Essentially, the service will aggregate data on carrier performance, transit delays and other service KPIs across supply chains and deliver a “FICO score for freight”. These metrics will help shippers and brokers understand performance at both the regional and individual carrier level – thus optimizing their selection of freight services and routes.

The revenues generated from the segment will be almost entirely fee-based. If Triumph is able to deliver actionable money-saving insights from the segment (the jury is still out since it’s been operating for less than a year), it should result in very valuable high-growth recurring revenues.

Management

Aaron Graft has been the CEO of Triumph since he and a group of investors acquired EJ Financial in 2010. He changed the name to Triumph and acquired Advance Business Capital, a leading factoring company, in 2012 in search of attractive returns for his bank’s deposit base. I don’t think Graft envisioned the company in its current form when he started down this path in 2010, but his incremental steps towards the company's form today have exhibited solid judgement. Under his leadership, the bank has grown from an asset base of $235 million to over $6 billion.

In my opinion, Graft’s leadership seems intentional and focused on long-term results. His shareholder letters attached to the company’s quarterly and annual reports are full of wisdom about the industry and espouse steady investment to achieve long-term gain rather than near-term margin expansion. Stock repurchases over the past five years seem to be focused on purchasing when shares were at depressed levels, which lends credibility to Graft’s skills as a capital allocator that understands the value of shareholder’s equity. Given this commitment to the business and the extended timeline which apparently informs his decisions, I was surprised to learn that he has a relatively small ownership stake. His 154k shares amount to less than 1% of the company and are currently valued at just over $8 million. Still, executive compensation is primarily based on Payments segment operating margins, Banking segment earnings growth and Factoring segment receivables, so Graft does at least seem to be financially incentivized to produce responsible long-term growth. His total compensation over the past three years has been in the neighborhood of $3 million, which seems reasonable considering what he’s done for the business. Still, I remain confused about why he hasn’t shared in the company’s success through a larger ownership position.

Valuation

Triumph may be viewed as two different businesses in one. One is a small regional bank with an industry-leading factoring business. The other is a freight services tech company which encompasses the payments and intelligence segments. The bank and factoring businesses are profitable, well run, and growing at a steady pace. The freight services tech segments, on the other hand, are more of a growth/tech offering, making their valuation a bit more opaque. Though the two are invariably intertwined, I’ll approach the tricky job of valuing this businesses from that angle to answer the following question: what is the value of the bank, and how much capital are we risking on the freight services tech company given that valuation?

Value of the Bank

The economic fundamentals of Triumph’s segments are all intertwined, which unfortunately makes valuing even the banking segment a tricky exercise. For instance, prior to the 2023 10-k, the banking segment included many corporate expenses including executive salaries along with certain SG&A expenses. Starting in 2023, however, many of these expenses were moved into the “Corporate” segment (which had existed before). Prior to this reclassification, the bank had been recording a very pedestrian efficiency ratio of around 70%. After the reclassification, the banking segment’s efficiency ratio has declined (lower is better) to around 50% - an excellent result. The company is changing rapidly so these adjustments may be appropriate. But it underscores the caution with which we need to approach this exercise.

With that caveat noted, we turn to the banking financials and find that both net interest income and non-interest income have been relatively flat since 2021, combining to generate about $245 million in net revenue over the period. Subtract expenses in line with their “new” combined ratio (I assume 51%), take out taxes and you arrive at bank earnings of about $90 million. Regional banks typically fetch a P/E ratio of around 13.5, which would value Triumph’s banking segment at about what the entire company trades for today - $1.2 billion. Triumph’s banking revenue has grown at a mid-teens CAGR over the past decade, so the value of the their banking unit in isolation should be valued at or above peer average. This valuation, of course, deserves an asterisk noting that many of the corporate expenses required to run the bank are house in a separate “corporate expenses” segment. For reference, the bank reported an average annual efficiency ratio of about 72% prior to the corporate expense migration, which would correspond to $50 million in earnings and a valuation of about $700 million.

Value of the Factoring Business

The factoring business has been a success story for Triumph overall, though it’s certainly hit a speedbump lately. The segment recorded sales of $91 million in 2019 before more than doubling that number to $210 million when freight rates spiked in 2022. Pre-tax earnings from the segment came in at $115 million that year, but have since fallen to about $30 million in each of the past two years.

The FRED chart below shows this recession in terms of pricing over a longer period.

It’s tough to say where this segment lands when things “even out,” but we can make a couple of educated guesses. While non-interest expense associated with the factoring segment has been in the low 70% range over the past two challenging years, it was in the low 40’s in the two booming years prior and in the mid-50’s in the two more moderate years before that. Given the fact that Triumph has launched a “Factoring as a Service” [FAAS] product, focused on helping factors operate more efficiently, it seems unlikely the factoring segment gets less efficient than it was before the pandemic. Even without assuming any revenue growth out of this freight recession, simply getting margins in line with current demand would increase pre-tax earnings in this segment to about $50 million.

While these numbers are very rough and approximate, I want to emphasize the conservatism behind them. This does not assume any revenue growth from their new FaaS offering, which has already been adopted by C.H. Robinson and RXO. Management stated an expectation for new revenue from the FaaS business to start growing meaningfully over the next few quarters. It also doesn’t assume any revenue growth or expansion in interest rate spreads, which are almost certain to happen at some point in this highly cyclical industry.

Valuation of the Payments and Intelligence Segments

I’m not going to put a numerical valuation on these segments because it would be too subjective. Triumph has created a one-of-a-kind payments network to serve the trucking industry and is at the beginning of its journey building out the intelligence segment. There isn’t really a precedent for these businesses on which to model their financials. Still, I’ll add some context using prior financial results and comments from management.

Triumph created today’s Payments segment after acquiring HubTran in 2021. Since then, the segment has grown at a 17% CAGR even in a tough trucking environment. EBITDA margins for Payments just crossed the “break-even” threshold in Q2, recording a value of 14%. Todd Ritterbusch, President of Payments and Banking, said in the company’s Q2, 2025 earnings call that he expected this metric to trend towards 40% over time, leaving plenty of room for margin expansion as the service expands. Net income is approaching breakeven, losing under $1 million in Q2 after revenue grew by 32% YOY. Payments should be contributing to the bottom line soon.

The Intelligence segment is more of a wildcard. Management is optimistic about its possibilities and guides towards a path similar to that taken by the Payments segment. The unit brought in $1.7 million in sales during Q2 and lost about $6 million on a pre-tax net income basis.

More important than any misguided assessment of these segment’s earnings power is an evaluation of their usefulness to the industry. If there is demand for these tools, profitability should take care of itself. And on that front I feel sanguine for a few reasons. First, as the Payments segment approaches profitability just 4 years after launch, Triumph has shown they understand the trucking industry’s pain points and can successfully bring a first of it’s kind product to the market and sell it to the participants. As a somewhat neutral party, their payments network has addressed headaches for shippers, brokers and carriers. The Intelligence segment offering has the same goal in mind. By leveraging the vast sea of data created in their Payments network (recall that it touches 50% of all brokered freight), they are in a great position to provide precise, real-time data to industry brokers. From a broker’s perspective, they can’t afford to let their competitors start using this tool to inform pricing and shipping decisions without securing access to that data themselves.

If sufficient demand for a product exists, the economy should eventually become profitable. As with any business built on technology and data, successful offerings should eventually reach a point of producing high gross margins with little capital. I think the tools offered in both the Payments and Intelligence segments will meet this bar.

Putting it all Together

My valuation of Triumph is a combination of the straightforward banking and factoring operations with the highly uncertain payments and intelligence segments. I estimate the company would generate about $125 million annually in net income if you were to carve out the banking and factoring segments. Add in the Corporate cost-center segment at minus $90 million net of tax, and you’re left with earnings of about $35 million.

But my assumptions are overly conservative in a number of ways. First, revenues and margins in the factoring segment are likely to rise as the freight industry exits its current recession and hauling prices start to rise. The FaaS service will also increase fee revenue and bring in additional factoring receivables. And while corporate expenses have grown at a rapid clip over the past few years, much of this has come from the need to support the growing payments and intelligence segments, which should produce enough revenue for the company to "grow into" that expense base.

Concerning the Payments and Intelligence segments, management is guiding towards 20% growth and has already started producing tangible results, including raising contract values on the GreenScreens.ai product from $37k pre-deal to $80k post-deal. Based on their Q2, 2025 investor presentation, they seem to think they’re just scratching the service.

Triumph’s valuation, to me, is quite concrete on a qualitative basis but very obscure quantitatively. Qualitatively, the company offers the only true payments network in the industry and collects more verified data than any other company. This well of data should be invaluable to brokers. Furthermore, the bank-backed Payments segment solves a lot of pain points for the industry in a way that other non-bank players can’t match.

From a quantitative standpoint, I don’t have a great way of putting a number on these products, which are in their infancy. If the company is successful in realizing their vision, I think the rewards will justify the company’s current valuation. The downside is limited to some degree by the company's well-run bank and factoring segments. Though the upside is hard to quantify, I think the business case for its success is apparent.

Risks

The most obvious risk to this investment is that their new business ventures fail to deliver on management’s lofty promises. Triumph is investing hundreds of millions of dollars into these new software services through both organic investment and acquisitions (GreenScreens.ai was acquired for $160 million). The current valuation, though down significantly from 2021 highs, still reflects big growth expectations.

The company also faces risk from the cyclical trucking industry. Should the freight recession continue on longer than expected, Triumph’s revenue growth will slow or even go into decline. Companies are much less likely to purchase expensive new software tools when they’re short on cashflow.

Triumph also faces credit risk, which might result in unexpected losses. Credit losses swelled from $7 million in 2022 to $19 million in 2024. This risk may also be compounded by a continuing freight recession, as many of their supply chain receivables are tied to the industry’s health.

Finally, Triumph faces risk in the form of changing interest rates. If interest rates rise in the future, the company’s deposit base may become more expensive and reduce the spread between factored invoice purchase prices and their cost of funds.

Summary

It is pleasing (though often misleading) to come away from investment analysis with a confident valuation. Sadly, that assured feeling has eluded me with Triumph. And yet, I still think the company should be a success story in the long run. The company's structural advantages are numerous, and capital allocation thus far has been sound. Though I can't tell you precisely how much Triumph is worth today, I will assert that it isn't egregiously overvalued. As the past two decades have shown us through "big tech" firms, purchasing a company with strong fundamental network effects even at prices that seem expensive today often ends up being highly successful investments. I expect the same to eventually prove true for Triumph.
